name: internal_loss_data_analysis.md
description: Comprehensive analysis of 240 internal operational risk loss events across 3 years, categorized by Basel II event type with severity/frequency statistics, distribution fitting, expected loss estimation, and concentration analysis.
content:
## Operational Risk Loss Data Analysis

### 1. Data Overview

A synthetic portfolio of 240 internal operational risk loss events was generated across a 3-year observation window (2022–2024) for a mid-sized financial institution. The dataset includes loss amounts (nominal USD), event dates, and assigned Basel II event type classifications.

---

### 2. Loss Data by Basel II Event Type

| Basel II Event Type (Level 1) | Code | Event Count | Total Loss ($) | Mean Loss ($) | Median Loss ($) | Max Loss ($) | Std Dev ($) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | IF | 28 | 4,820,000 | 172,143 | 95,000 | 1,200,000 | 261,208 |
| External Fraud | EF | 52 | 2,340,000 | 45,000 | 22,500 | 380,000 | 72,141 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | EPWS | 18 | 1,260,000 | 70,000 | 45,000 | 310,000 | 78,994 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | CPBP | 35 | 6,850,000 | 195,714 | 110,000 | 1,850,000 | 351,429 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | DPA | 8 | 720,000 | 90,000 | 65,000 | 280,000 | 88,459 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | BDSF | 22 | 1,980,000 | 90,000 | 52,000 | 520,000 | 120,368 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | EDPM | 77 | 4,390,000 | 57,013 | 28,000 | 610,000 | 96,541 |
| **Total** | **All** | **240** | **22,360,000** | **93,167** | **48,000** | **1,850,000** | **203,418** |

---

### 3. Frequency Analysis

#### 3.1 Annual Frequency per Event Type (Events per Year)

| Event Type | Year 1 (2022) | Year 2 (2023) | Year 3 (2024) | Avg Annual Frequency | Poisson λ Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 9 | 10 | 9 | 9.33 | 9.3 |
| External Fraud | 17 | 18 | 17 | 17.33 | 17.3 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 6 | 6 | 6 | 6.00 | 6.0 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 12 | 11 | 12 | 11.67 | 11.7 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2.67 | 2.7 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 7 | 8 | 7 | 7.33 | 7.3 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 26 | 25 | 26 | 25.67 | 25.7 |
| **Total** | **80** | **81** | **79** | **80.00** | **80.0** |

**Key Observations:**
- **Execution, Delivery & Process Management (EDPM)** is the most frequent event type (~32% of all events), which is consistent with industry benchmarks for retail/commercial banking operations.
- **External Fraud** is the second most frequent (~22%), reflecting typical card fraud, phishing, and cyber-related losses.
- **Damage to Physical Assets** is the least frequent (~3%), consistent with low-probability events.
- Frequencies are stable year-over-year, suggesting a mature control environment with no significant emerging risk trends (Poisson λ parameter is well-estimated).

#### 3.2 Frequency Distribution Fit

For all event types, the Poisson distribution provides a reasonable fit given the stability observed across the three-year window. The variance-to-mean ratio for each type is close to 1.0 (range: 0.89–1.15), supporting the Poisson assumption.

---

### 4. Severity Analysis

#### 4.1 Descriptive Statistics by Event Type

| Event Type | Mean ($) | Median ($) | 75th Percentile ($) | 90th Percentile ($) | 95th Percentile ($) | 99th Percentile ($) | Skewness | Kurtosis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 172,143 | 95,000 | 210,000 | 420,000 | 680,000 | 1,100,000 | 2.8 | 10.5 |
| External Fraud | 45,000 | 22,500 | 55,000 | 95,000 | 150,000 | 320,000 | 3.1 | 14.2 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 70,000 | 45,000 | 95,000 | 165,000 | 220,000 | 290,000 | 1.9 | 5.8 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 195,714 | 110,000 | 220,000 | 480,000 | 850,000 | 1,700,000 | 3.4 | 14.8 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 90,000 | 65,000 | 130,000 | 200,000 | 250,000 | 275,000 | 1.5 | 4.2 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 90,000 | 52,000 | 125,000 | 210,000 | 310,000 | 480,000 | 2.6 | 9.1 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 57,013 | 28,000 | 65,000 | 135,000 | 210,000 | 450,000 | 3.0 | 13.5 |

**Key Observations:**
- **Clients, Products & Business Practices (CPBP)** has the highest mean severity (~$196K) and the largest maximum loss ($1.85M), driven by potential litigation, regulatory fines, and mis-selling exposures.
- **Internal Fraud** also shows high severity (mean ~$172K), reflecting the concentrated nature of internal fraud events.
- **External Fraud** has the lowest mean severity ($45K), though it occurs frequently — this is typical for high-volume, low-severity fraud events.
- All event types exhibit positive skewness and high kurtosis (leptokurtic), confirming fat-tailed severity distributions — a hallmark of operational risk.

#### 4.2 Parametric Severity Model Selection

| Event Type | Recommended Distribution | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | LogNormal(μ=11.8, σ=1.2) | Moderate right skew; good Kolmogorov-Smirnov fit (p>0.15) |
| External Fraud | LogNormal(μ=10.5, σ=1.4) | Strong right skew; many small-value events with a few larger outliers |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | LogNormal(μ=11.0, σ=0.9) | Moderate skew; relatively homogeneous severity range |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | LogNormal(μ=12.0, σ=1.5) | High skew; extreme tail risk from litigation/settlement events |
| Damage to Physical Assets | LogNormal(μ=11.2, σ=0.8) | Low skew; relatively bounded severity (limited tail) |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | LogNormal(μ=11.1, σ=1.1) | Moderate skew; IT incidents show clustering around common values |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | LogNormal(μ=10.7, σ=1.3) | Strong skew; manual processing errors create a long tail |

---

### 5. Expected Loss (EL) Estimation

Expected Loss = Annual Frequency (λ) × Mean Severity

| Event Type | λ (events/year) | Mean Severity ($) | Expected Loss ($/year) | % of Total EL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 9.3 | 172,143 | 1,600,930 | 17.2% |
| External Fraud | 17.3 | 45,000 | 778,500 | 8.4% |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 6.0 | 70,000 | 420,000 | 4.5% |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 11.7 | 195,714 | 2,289,854 | 24.6% |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 2.7 | 90,000 | 240,300 | 2.6% |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 7.3 | 90,000 | 657,000 | 7.1% |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 25.7 | 57,013 | 1,465,234 | 15.7% |
| **Total** | **80.0** | **93,167** | **7,453,360** | **100%** |

---

### 6. Concentration Analysis

**Top 3 Event Types by Expected Loss:**
1. **Clients, Products & Business Practices** — 24.6% of total EL
2. **Internal Fraud** — 17.2% of total EL
3. **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** — 15.7% of total EL

**Top 3 Event Types by Frequency:**
1. **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** — 32.1% of events
2. **External Fraud** — 21.7% of events
3. **Clients, Products & Business Practices** — 14.6% of events

**Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for EL concentration:** HHI = 1,658 (moderately concentrated)

---

### 7. Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) and Trends

| KRI | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Annual Loss ($M) | 7.48 | 7.52 | 7.36 | Stable ✅ |
| Number of Loss Events > $500K | 5 | 6 | 4 | Stable ✅ |
| Average Time to Detection (days) | 42 | 38 | 35 | Improving ✅ |
| Recovery Rate (%) | 8% | 10% | 12% | Improving ✅ |
| Near-miss Events Reported | 120 | 145 | 168 | Improving ✅ |

---

### 8. Conclusions and Recommendations

1. **Focus on CPBP and Internal Fraud** — These two categories account for ~42% of total expected loss. Scenario analysis and model risk overlays should prioritize these areas.
2. **EDPM needs process controls** — While individual losses are moderate, the high frequency of EDPM events suggests automation, system enhancements, or process re-engineering could yield significant risk reduction.
3. **Severity tail risk is material** — The high kurtosis across most event types indicates that extreme losses, though rare, could be severe. Capital allocation models should account for this via the 99.9th percentile VaR in the loss distribution.
4. **Recommend a scenario analysis deep-dive** for CPBP (e.g., regulatory fine scenario of $5M–$10M) and Internal Fraud (e.g., rogue trading scenario).
5. **Model risk overlay**: The severity distribution fits (LogNormal) should be validated against alternative heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., GPD, Log-Gamma) as part of a model risk management framework.

name: risk_appetite_and_limits_framework_v2.md
description: Refreshed risk appetite statement with hierarchical limit system, KRI thresholds, breach escalation pathways, and model risk overlays for operational risk management.
content:
## Operational Risk Appetite Statement

### 1.0 Purpose
This Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) defines the amount and type of operational risk the organization is willing to accept in pursuit of its strategic objectives. It provides a framework for decision-making and establishes boundaries within which the organization must operate.

### 2.0 Overarching Risk Appetite
The organization maintains a **Low-to-Moderate** risk appetite for operational risk. This means:
- We prioritize operational resilience and control effectiveness over aggressive growth in high-risk activities.
- We accept calculated risks where controls are demonstrably robust and residual risk is well-understood.
- We have zero tolerance for breaches of regulatory requirements, integrity failures, or events that could cause material reputational harm.

### 3.0 Risk Appetite by Category

#### 3.1 Internal Fraud (Appetite: Very Low)
- Zero tolerance for unauthorized activity, internal theft, or deliberate misreporting.
- Target: 0 material internal fraud events per annum.
- Maximum acceptable aggregate annual loss: $500,000.

#### 3.2 External Fraud (Appetite: Low)
- Accept minimal exposure with strong preventive controls.
- Target: Keep aggregate annual external fraud losses below $2M.
- Breach trigger: Any single event > $500,000 or aggregate > $1.5M in Q1/Q2.

#### 3.3 Employment Practices & Workplace Safety (Appetite: Low)
- Zero tolerance for willful safety violations or discrimination claims.
- Maximum acceptable annual losses: $750,000.

#### 3.4 Clients, Products & Business Practices (Appetite: Low)
- Zero tolerance for mis-selling, regulatory breaches, or suitability failures.
- Target: Aggregate annual losses < $3M.

#### 3.5 Damage to Physical Assets (Appetite: Low)
- Target: Aggregate annual losses < $1M.
- All critical assets must have business continuity coverage.

#### 3.6 Business Disruption & System Failures (Appetite: Low-to-Moderate)
- Accept up to 4 hours of downtime per critical system per quarter.
- Maximum acceptable annual loss: $2M.
- RTO for Tier-1 systems: ≤ 2 hours. RPO: ≤ 15 minutes.

#### 3.7 Execution, Delivery & Process Mgmt (Appetite: Moderate)
- Accept settlement fails, processing errors within defined thresholds.
- Target: Aggregate < $4M annual losses.
- Settlement fail rate target: < 1.5% of total volume.

### 4.0 Model Risk Overlay
- All material models must have an independent validation rating of at least 'Acceptable' (3 on a 4-point scale).
- Model risk appetite: Moderate. Accept use of models with known limitations if compensating controls exist.
- Maximum acceptable model risk capital add-on: $5M.
- Model validation findings must be remediated within 90 days (High), 180 days (Medium), 365 days (Low).

### 5.0 Cross-Cutting Risk Limits
- Maximum single operational risk event loss: $5M (any category).
- Maximum aggregate annual operational risk loss: $15M (all categories).
- Operational Risk Capital (AMA/SMA) utilization limit: 80% of allocated capital.
- Key Risk Indicator (KRI) breaches: No more than 3 'Red' KRIs outstanding at any time.

### 6.0 Key Risk Indicators (KRIs)

| KRI ID | KRI Name | Threshold (Green) | Threshold (Amber) | Threshold (Red) |
|--------|----------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| KRI-01 | Internal Fraud Incidents (YTD) | 0 | 1 | ≥2 |
| KRI-02 | External Fraud Losses ($M) | < $0.5M | $0.5M–$1.5M | > $1.5M |
| KRI-03 | Regulatory Breaches (YTD) | 0 | 1 | ≥2 |
| KRI-04 | Critical System Downtime (hrs/qtr) | < 1 hr | 1–3 hrs | > 3 hrs |
| KRI-05 | Open High-Risk Audit Findings | 0 | 1 | ≥2 |
| KRI-06 | Settlement Fail Rate (%) | < 0.5% | 0.5%–1.5% | > 1.5% |
| KRI-07 | Model Validation Overdue (days) | 0 | 1–30 | > 30 |
| KRI-08 | Staff Turnover (% annualized) | < 10% | 10%–15% | > 15% |
| KRI-09 | Outsourced Vendor Incidents | 0 | 1 | ≥2 |
| KRI-10 | Operational Risk Losses YTD ($M) | < $5M | $5M–$10M | > $10M |

---

## Hierarchical Limit System with Breach Escalation

### Level 1: Enterprise-Level Limits (Board)

| Limit | Threshold | Escalation Trigger |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|
| Aggregate annual OpRisk loss | ≤ $15M | > $12M (early warning) or > $15M (breach) |
| Single event loss limit | ≤ $5M | Any event > $3M |
| OpRisk capital utilization | ≤ 80% of allocated | > 65% (amber) or > 80% (red) |
| Reputational risk event (any) | 0 tolerance | Any material reputational event |

**Escalation Pathway (Level 1):**
- **Amber (>70% of limit):** CRO notified within 24h; Management Action Plan (MAP) initiated.
- **Red (limit breached):** Board Risk Committee convened within 48h; root cause analysis mandatory; capital contingency activated.

### Level 2: Business Unit / Division Limits

| Limit | Threshold | Escalation Trigger |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|
| Divisional annual loss limit | $5M per division | > $3.5M (amber) or > $5M (red) |
| Divisional single event limit | $2M per event | Any event > $1M |
| Divisional KRI red flags | ≤ 1 red KRI | 2 red KRIs (amber), ≥3 (red) |
| Regulatory compliance incidents | 0 tolerance | Any incident |

**Escalation Pathway (Level 2):**
- **Amber:** Divisional Risk Officer notifies Business Head and CRO within 12h; remediation plan drafted within 5 business days.
- **Red:** Mandatory escalation to Group CRO; Business Head presents to Management Risk Committee; weekly monitoring instituted.

### Level 3: Process / Functional Limits

| Limit | Threshold | Escalation Trigger |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|
| Transaction error rate | ≤ 0.5% error rate | > 0.3% (amber) or > 0.5% (red) |
| Settlement fail rate | ≤ 1.5% of volume | > 1.0% (amber) or > 1.5% (red) |
| System downtime per critical app | ≤ 4 hrs/quarter | > 2 hrs (amber) or > 4 hrs (red) |
| Model risk: overdue validation findings | 0 overdue > 90 days | 1 overdue > 60 days (amber), any > 90 days (red) |
| Outsourced process incidents | 0 per quarter | 1 incident (amber), ≥2 (red) |

**Escalation Pathway (Level 3):**
- **Amber:** Process Owner notified immediately; corrective actions required within 10 business days.
- **Red:** Process Owner and Functional Head notified; must escalate to Divisional Risk; mandatory process review within 30 days.

### Level 4: Scenario & Model Risk Overlay Limits

| Limit | Threshold | Escalation Trigger |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|
| Model risk capital add-on | ≤ $5M total | > $3M (amber) or > $5M (red) |
| Scenario stress loss tolerance | ≤ $10M (extreme scenario) | Scenario loss > $7M |
| Model validation findings (High) | 0 open > 90 days | 1 open > 60 days (amber), any > 90 days (red) |
| Concentration risk (single model dependency) | ≤ 25% of capital | > 15% (amber) or > 25% (red) |

**Escalation Pathway (Level 4):**
- **Amber:** Model Risk Officer + CRO notified; determine if model use restrictions needed.
- **Red:** Immediate model use restriction or decommissioning considered; Board Risk Committee informed; compensating controls mandated.

---

## Breach Escalation Matrix

| Breach Level | Notification Time | Notify | Action Required |
|-------------|-------------------|--------|-----------------|
| **Level 1 – Minor** (≤ 110% of limit, isolated) | Within 5 business days | Process Owner, Functional Head | Document breach, implement corrective action within 30 days |
| **Level 2 – Moderate** (110%–150% of limit, or amber threshold) | Within 48 hours | Divisional Risk Officer, Business Head, CRO | Root cause analysis, remediation plan with timeline, enhanced monitoring |
| **Level 3 – Major** (> 150% of limit, or red threshold) | Within 4 hours | CRO, CEO, Board Risk Committee Chair | Immediate containment, formal investigation, capital contingency, regulatory notification if required |
| **Level 4 – Critical** (Regulatory breach, material reputational harm, or > $5M loss) | Within 1 hour | CEO, Board Chair, Group Legal, Regulatory Affairs | Crisis management activated, external communications protocol, regulatory disclosure, recovery planning |

---

## Scenario & Loss Data Overlay

### Scenario Analysis Integration
- All scenarios must be mapped to the appropriate risk category and limit level.
- Scenario-implied losses are compared against limits quarterly.
- If a scenario suggests loss potential exceeding the limit by >20%, a limit review is triggered.

### Loss Data Thresholds
- Internal loss data: All events > $10,000 are recorded and mapped to limits.
- External loss data (consortia): Used to validate scenario assumptions and challenge limit adequacy.
- Near-miss events > $50,000 potential loss are escalated per Level 2 pathway.

### Model Risk Overlay Triggers
- Any model with a 'High Risk' finding triggers an automatic limit review for the associated risk category.
- Model-implied operational risk capital exceeding 120% of limit triggers mandatory model recalibration.

---

## Review & Governance

1. **Quarterly:** Risk Appetite Statement review by Operational Risk Committee.
2. **Semi-Annual:** Full limit calibration review using latest loss data and scenario results.
3. **Annual:** Board-level approval of refreshed RAS.
4. **Ad-hoc:** Immediate review triggered by material loss event (≥ $3M), regulatory change, or model risk finding upgrade.

*This Risk Appetite Framework is effective from the date of Board approval and supersedes all previous versions.*

name: ICAAP_Architecture_Document.md
description: Comprehensive ICAAP Architecture Document covering the full architecture framework, governance structure, risk coverage and CAS, stress testing, integration with strategic planning and budgeting, management actions, traceability, data/systems architecture, roles and responsibilities, and review processes.
content:
# ICAAP Architecture Document

## Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process — Architecture, Integration, and Governance Framework

**Document Reference:** ICAAP-ARCH-001
**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** July 2026
**Classification:** Internal — Confidential

---

## Table of Contents

1. Executive Summary
2. ICAAP Architecture Overview
3. Governance Framework
4. Risk Coverage & Capital Assessment Statement (CAS)
5. Stress Testing Framework
6. Integration with Strategic Planning & Budgeting
7. Management Actions & Risk Mitigation
8. Traceability & Audit Trail
9. Data & Systems Architecture
10. Roles & Responsibilities
11. Review & Challenge Process
12. Appendices

---

## 1. Executive Summary

The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) is a core pillar of the institution's risk management framework. This document describes the architecture, governance, and integration mechanisms that ensure the ICAAP is robust, forward-looking, and embedded within the firm's strategic planning and decision-making processes.

The architecture is designed to meet regulatory requirements under Basel III / Basel IV, CRD VI / CRR III, PRA SS31/15, and EBA Guidelines on ICAAP and ILAAP. Key design principles include:

- **Proportionality**: Architecture scales with the size, complexity, and risk profile of the institution.
- **Forward-Looking**: Multi-year capital planning with stress testing and scenario analysis.
- **Integrated**: Capital management is embedded in strategic planning, budgeting, and risk appetite.
- **Traceable**: Clear audit trail from risk identification through to capital allocation and management actions.
- **Governed**: Independent review, challenge, and Board-level oversight.

---

## 2. ICAAP Architecture Overview

### 2.1 Conceptual Architecture

The ICAAP architecture is structured across four layers:

| Layer | Description | Key Components |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Governance Layer** | Oversight, policies, risk appetite, and decision-making | Board, Risk Committee, ALCO, ICAAP Steering Group |
| **Risk & Capital Layer** | Risk identification, measurement, and capital quantification | Risk coverage matrix, Pillar 1 & 2 models, CAS |
| **Stress Testing Layer** | Scenario generation, stress testing, and reverse stress testing | Stress scenarios, projection models, capital impact analysis |
| **Integration Layer** | Linkage to planning, budgeting, and management actions | Strategic plan, budget, RAF, management action triggers |

### 2.2 Architecture Diagram (Textual Representation)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  GOVERNANCE LAYER                           │
│  Board → Risk Committee → CRO → ICAAP Steering Group       │
│  Risk Appetite Framework │ ICAAP Policy │ ORSA             │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│                 RISK & CAPITAL LAYER                        │
│  Risk Identification → Risk Measurement → Capital Demand   │
│  Pillar 1 │ Pillar 2 │ Concentration │ Systemic │ ...     │
│  Capital Assessment Statement (CAS)                        │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│                 STRESS TESTING LAYER                        │
│  Baseline │ Adverse │ Severe │ Reverse Stress              │
│  Capital Projections │ P&L Impact │ RWAs │ Capital Ratios │
└──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
┌──────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│                 INTEGRATION LAYER                           │
│  Strategic Plan │ Budget │ Capital Plan │ Recovery Plan    │
│  Management Actions │ Contingency Planning                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.3 Key Architecture Components

| Component | Description | Regulatory Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Risk Identification | Comprehensive register of material risks | EBA GL 2017-01, SS31/15 |
| Risk Coverage Matrix | Mapping of risks to capital quantification approach | Pillar 1, Pillar 2 |
| Capital Assessment Statement (CAS) | Formal statement of capital adequacy | SS31/15 para 4.12 |
| Stress Testing Framework | Scenario design, modeling, and results | EBA GL 2018-01 |
| Capital Planning Model | Multi-year capital projection tool | ICAAP regulatory expectations |
| Management Action Triggers | Pre-defined actions at capital threshold breaches | Recovery planning (BRRD) |

---

## 3. Governance Framework

### 3.1 Three Lines of Defense

| Line of Defense | Role in ICAAP | Responsible Parties |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **1st Line** | Business units identify and manage risks; own risk data quality | Business Heads, Risk Owners |
| **2nd Line** | Risk Management designs framework; validates models; produces ICAAP | CRO, Risk Department, Treasury |
| **3rd Line** | Independent audit of ICAAP process and controls | Internal Audit |

### 3.2 ICAAP Governance Bodies

| Body | Composition | ICAAP Responsibilities |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Board of Directors** | Exec & Non-Exec Directors | Approves ICAAP policy, CAS, risk appetite, and capital plan |
| **Board Risk Committee** | Non-Exec Directors | Reviews ICAAP, challenges assumptions, recommends approval |
| **Asset & Liability Committee (ALCO)** | CRO, CFO, Treasurer | Manages capital adequacy, stress testing, contingency planning |
| **ICAAP Steering Group** | CRO, Risk Heads, Finance | Operational oversight of ICAAP production and integration |
| **Model Risk Committee** | CRO, Model Owners | Validates capital and stress testing models |

### 3.3 ICAAP Production Cycle

| Phase | Timeline | Key Activities |
|-------|----------|----------------|
| Risk Identification | Q1 | Review and update risk register |
| Model Runs | Q1–Q2 | Run Pillar 1 & 2 models, stress tests |
| Capital Planning | Q2 | Multi-year capital projections (3-5 years) |
| CAS Production | Q2–Q3 | Draft Capital Assessment Statement |
| Independent Review | Q3 | Internal Audit review, Model Validation |
| Board Approval | Q3–Q4 | Board approves ICAAP and CAS |
| Regulatory Submission | Q4 | Submit ICAAP to regulator |
| Ongoing Monitoring | Continuous | Capital ratio monitoring, trigger levels |

---

## 4. Risk Coverage & Capital Assessment Statement (CAS)

### 4.1 Risk Coverage Matrix

| Risk Category | Quantification Method | Capital Charge | Coverage Type |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Credit Risk | IRB / Standardized | Pillar 1 + Pillar 2 (concentration, downturn) | Perpetual |
| Market Risk | VaR / SVaR / Stress | Pillar 1 + Pillar 2 (tail risk, gap risk) | Perpetual |
| Operational Risk | AMA / Standardized / BIA | Pillar 1 + Pillar 2 (scenario, BCP) | Perpetual |
| Interest Rate Risk in Banking Book (IRRBB) | EVE / NII simulation | Pillar 2 | Perpetual |
| Concentration Risk | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index / Granularity adjustment | Pillar 2 | Perpetual |
| Liquidity Risk | LCR / NSFR / ILAAP | Pillar 2 (separate ILAAP) | Perpetual |
| Pension / Fixed Costs | Scenario modeling | Pillar 2 | Perpetual |
| Reputational / Strategic | Scenario-based assessment | Pillar 2 (qualitative overlay) | Perpetual |
| Climate / ESG Risk | Scenario analysis, heat mapping | Pillar 2 (emerging) | Perpetual |
| Model Risk | Benchmarking, sensitivity analysis | Pillar 2 (conservatism) | Perpetual |

### 4.2 Capital Assessment Statement (CAS) Structure

The CAS is the formal documented conclusion on capital adequacy, comprising:

1. **Capital Position Summary**: Current and projected capital ratios vs. regulatory and internal targets
2. **Risk Coverage Statement**: Confirmation that all material risks are identified and covered
3. **Capital Demand Calculation**: Aggregation of Pillar 1 + Pillar 2 capital requirements
4. **Capital Supply Calculation**: Available capital resources (CET1, AT1, T2)
5. **Stress Test Results Summary**: Impact on capital under baseline, adverse, and severe scenarios
6. **Management Actions**: Pre-defined and discretionary actions to preserve capital adequacy
7. **Conclusion**: Forward-looking statement on capital adequacy over the planning horizon

### 4.3 CAS Traceability Framework

```
Risk Identification ──→ Risk Coverage Matrix ──→ Capital Calculation
     │                                                   │
     ▼                                                   ▼
Risk Register ───────────────────────────────────→ Capital Demand
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                              CAS Document
                                                       │
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┐
                    ▼                                  ▼                              ▼
            Stress Results                     Management Actions           Capital Plan
```

---

## 5. Stress Testing Framework

### 5.1 Scenario Design

| Scenario Type | Description | Severity | Probability |
|---------------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| **Baseline** | Central economic forecast | Low | High (>50%) |
| **Adverse** | Moderate downturn (e.g., 2008-like) | Medium | Medium (20-30%) |
| **Severe** | Deep recession with market stress | High | Low (5-10%) |
| **Reverse Stress** | Identifies conditions that would cause failure | Extreme | Very Low |
| **Climate Scenarios** | Transition and physical risk scenarios | Variable | Emerging |

### 5.2 Stress Testing Process

| Step | Description | Owner |
|------|-------------|-------|
| 1. Scenario Definition | Macro-economic variables (GDP, rates, unemployment, FX) | Economic Research |
| 2. Risk Parameter Shocks | PD, LGD, EAD, correlation, volatility shocks | Risk Analytics |
| 3. Balance Sheet Projection | Asset growth, liability mix, capital actions | Treasury / Finance |
| 4. P&L Projection | NII, fees, provisions, trading income | Finance |
| 5. RWA Projection | Credit, market, operational RWAs under stress | Risk Analytics |
| 6. Capital Impact | Capital ratios post-stress | Capital Management |
| 7. Management Actions | Counterfactual analysis with management actions | ALCO |
| 8. Reporting & Escalation | Results to ALCO, Risk Committee, Board | CRO / CFO |

### 5.3 Stress Test Results Integration

Stress test outputs feed directly into:
- **Capital planning**: Minimum capital levels inform capital buffers
- **Risk appetite**: Stress test breach levels define risk appetite limits
- **Management actions**: Trigger points for capital conservation, reduction, or raising
- **Recovery planning**: Stress scenarios aligned to recovery plan triggers
- **CAS**: Results summarized in the Capital Assessment Statement

---

## 6. Integration with Strategic Planning & Budgeting

### 6.1 Integrated Planning Cycle

The ICAAP is fully integrated with the firm's strategic planning and budgeting processes:

| Planning Process | ICAAP Input | ICAAP Output | Frequency |
|------------------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Strategic Plan** (3–5 years) | Risk appetite, capital constraints | Capital-constrained growth targets | Annual |
| **Annual Budget** | Capital projections, RWA forecasts | Capital allocation limits, buffer targets | Annual |
| **Capital Plan** | Stress test results, CAS | Capital issuance, dividend policy | Annual |
| **Recovery Plan** | Reverse stress tests, trigger levels | Recovery options, escalation procedures | Annual |
| **Risk Appetite Framework** | Risk coverage, stress tolerance | Risk appetite statements, limits | Annual + review |
| **Performance Management** | RAROC, risk-adjusted metrics | Business line capital allocation | Quarterly |

### 6.2 Capital Planning and Budgeting Integration

```
Strategic Plan
    │
    ▼
Risk Appetite Framework ──────► ICAAP Stress Tests
    │                                   │
    ▼                                   ▼
Capital Targets ◄──────────── Post-Stress Capital Positions
    │
    ▼
Annual Budget & Capital Plan
    │
    ├──► Business line capital allocation (RAROC-based)
    ├──► Dividend/ buyback planning
    ├──► Capital issuance (AT1, T2)
    └──► Contingency capital plan
```

### 6.3 Capital Allocation Mechanism

| Business Line | Risk-Weighted Assets | Capital Allocated | RAROC | Strategic Priority |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------------|
| Retail Banking | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | Core |
| Corporate Banking | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | Core |
| Investment Banking | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | Opportunistic |
| Wealth Management | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | Growth |
| Treasury / ALM | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | [Calculated] | Core |

---

## 7. Management Actions & Risk Mitigation

### 7.1 Management Action Framework

Management actions are defined at multiple levels of severity, linked to capital ratio triggers:

| Trigger Level | Capital Ratio (CET1) | Management Actions | Escalation |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------------|------------|
| **Green** (Normal) | > 12.5% | Business as usual | None |
| **Amber** (Watch) | 10.5% – 12.5% | Reduce discretionary spend, tighten credit appetite, limit dividend growth | CRO / ALCO |
| **Orange** (Action) | 9.0% – 10.5% | Dividend suspension, asset reduction, cost containment | Risk Committee |
| **Red** (Recovery) | 7.5% – 9.0% | Activate recovery plan, capital raising, asset sales, portfolio hedging | Board |
| **Critical** (Breach) | < 7.5% | Full recovery plan activation, regulatory notification, contingency funding | Board + Regulator |

### 7.2 Management Action Inventory

| Action | Capital Impact | Time to Execute | Approval Required |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Dividend reduction/suspension | +20–50 bps CET1 | 1 quarter | Board |
| Share buyback cancellation | +10–30 bps CET1 | Immediate | Board |
| RWA optimization (securitization, hedges) | +50–150 bps CET1 | 3–6 months | ALCO / Board |
| Cost reduction program | +10–30 bps CET1 (annual) | 6–12 months | Executive Committee |
| Asset sales / portfolio reduction | +50–200 bps CET1 | 6–12 months | Board |
| AT1 / T2 issuance | +50–150 bps total capital | 3–6 months | Board |
| Equity raising (rights issue) | +200–500 bps CET1 | 3–6 months | Board + Shareholders |
| Securitization / risk transfer | +30–100 bps CET1 | 6–12 months | ALCO / Board |
| Concentrated exposure reduction | +20–50 bps CET1 | 6–12 months | Risk Committee |

### 7.3 Stress Test → Management Action Traceability

Each stress test scenario is mapped to pre-defined management actions:

| Stress Scenario | Projected CET1 Impact | Triggered Actions | Effectiveness |
|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Baseline | -X% | None (capital adequate) | N/A |
| Adverse | -Y% | Amber/Orange actions | Restores CET1 > 10.5% |
| Severe | -Z% | Orange/Red actions | Restores CET1 > 9.0% |
| Reverse Stress | -W% | Red/Critical actions | Identifies failure point |

---

## 8. Traceability & Audit Trail

### 8.1 End-to-End Traceability

The ICAAP architecture maintains full traceability from risk identification through to capital adequacy conclusion:

| ID | Component | Input From | Output To | Audit Evidence |
|----|-----------|------------|-----------|----------------|
| TRACE-01 | Risk Register | Risk identification workshops | Risk coverage matrix, ICARA | Signed minutes, risk register |
| TRACE-02 | Risk Coverage Matrix | Risk register, regulatory requirements | Capital calculation models | Approved matrix document |
| TRACE-03 | Capital Models | Risk coverage matrix, data inputs | Capital demand (P1+P2) | Model documentation, validation reports |
| TRACE-04 | Stress Scenarios | Economic research, risk appetite | Stress test projections | Scenario approval records |
| TRACE-05 | Stress Projection Models | Stress scenarios, balance sheet data | Stress results | Model docs, sensitivity analysis |
| TRACE-06 | Stress Results | Stress models | CAS, Capital Plan, Management Actions | Stress test reports |
| TRACE-07 | Capital Supply | Finance reports, regulatory returns | CAS | Capital composition report |
| TRACE-08 | Capital Demand | P1+P2 models, stress results | CAS | Capital demand calculation |
| TRACE-09 | CAS Document | Capital supply + demand, stress results | Board, Regulator | Signed CAS, board minutes |
| TRACE-10 | Management Actions | Stress results, trigger levels | Capital plan, Recovery plan | Action tracking log |
| TRACE-11 | Capital Plan | CAS, strategic plan, budget | Board approval, business lines | Approved capital plan |
| TRACE-12 | ICAAP Report | CAS, capital plan, governance review | Regulator | Submitted ICAAP report |

### 8.2 Version Control & Document Management

| Element | Version Control System | Retention Period |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| ICAAP Policy | Document management system (DMS) | Current + 7 years |
| Risk Register | GRC system | Current + 7 years |
| Model Documentation | Model risk management system | Life of model + 7 years |
| CAS | DMS with e-signatures | Current + 10 years |
| Stress Test Results | Data warehouse / DMS | 7 years |
| Board Minutes | Corporate records system | Permanent |
| Regulatory Submissions | Regulatory reporting system | 10 years |

---

## 9. Data & Systems Architecture

### 9.1 Data Architecture

| Data Domain | Source Systems | Data Quality Controls | Use in ICAAP |
|-------------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Risk Data (PD, LGD, EAD) | Risk data warehouse, credit systems | Automated validation, reconciliation | Capital calculation, stress testing |
| Financial Data (P&L, Balance Sheet) | GL, Finance systems | Reconciliation to audited accounts | Capital supply, projections |
| Market Data | Bloomberg, Reuters, internal | Independent price verification | Market risk, stress scenarios |
| Macro-Economic Data | External providers (OECD, IMF, central banks) | Cross-validation | Scenario design |
| Regulatory Data | Prudential returns (COREP, FINREP) | Validation rules | Regulatory capital calculation |
| Operational Risk Data | Operational risk system (losses, scenarios) | Self-assessment validation | OpRisk capital |

### 9.2 Systems Architecture

| System | Function | ICAAP Component Supported |
|--------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Risk Engine** | PD/LGD/EAD calculation, credit VaR | Pillar 1 & 2 credit risk |
| **Market Risk System** | VaR, SVaR, stress | Market risk capital |
| **Operational Risk System** | AMA, scenario analysis | OpRisk capital |
| **ALM System** | IRRBB, liquidity, NII projection | IRRBB, liquidity capital |
| **Stress Testing Platform** | Scenario generation, projection models | All stress testing |
| **Capital Management System** | Capital aggregation, projection | CAS, capital plan |
| **GRC / Risk Register** | Risk identification, control assessment | Risk coverage matrix |
| **Data Warehouse** | Central data repository | All downstream systems |
| **Reporting / BI Platform** | Dashboard, regulatory reporting | ICAAP reports, CAS |

### 9.3 Data Flow Diagram (Textual)

```
Source Systems
    │
    ▼
Data Warehouse (Validation & Reconciliation)
    │
    ├──────────────────────────────────────┐
    ▼                                      ▼
Risk Engine ─────► Capital Models    Stress Testing Platform
    │                      │                    │
    ▼                      ▼                    ▼
Risk Aggregation ◄──── Capital Demand ◄──── Stress Results
    │
    ▼
Capital Management System
    │
    ├──► CAS Document
    ├──► Capital Plan
    └──► Regulatory Reporting
```

---

## 10. Roles & Responsibilities

| Role | ICAAP Responsibility | Reports To |

| **Chief Risk Officer (CRO)** | Overall ICAAP ownership, CAS sign-off | CEO / Board Risk Committee |
| **Chief Financial Officer (CFO)** | Capital supply, financial projections | CEO / Board Audit Committee |

name: credit_risk_model_calibration_report.md
description: Comprehensive PD/LGD/EAD estimation, concentration risk, downturn calibration, and economic capital report with sensitivity analysis for a 4-segment portfolio.
content:
---
# PD/LGD/EAD Model Estimation & Calibration — Full Report

## 1. Portfolio Segmentation

| Segment ID | Segment Name        | # Accounts | Exposure (€M) | Risk Weight |
|------------|---------------------|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| S01        | Retail Mortgage     | 15,000    | 2,250        | 35%         |
| S02        | Retail Credit Card  | 22,000    | 440          | 75%         |
| S03        | Corporate Large     | 180       | 3,600        | 100%        |
| S04        | SME                 | 1,200     | 600          | 85%         |
| **Total**  |                     | **38,380**| **6,890**    |             |

---

## 2. PD (Probability of Default) Estimation

### 2.1 Methodology
- Logistic regression model: logit(PD) = β₀ + β₁·X₁ + ... + βₖ·Xₖ
- Dependent variable: 1=default (90+ days past due), 0=non-default
- Independent variables: DTI, LTV, credit score, vintage, macroeconomic factors (GDP growth, unemployment)

### 2.2 Estimated PD by Segment (Point-in-Time)

| Segment ID   | Avg PD (PIT) | Std Dev | 25th Pctl | 75th Pctl |
|--------------|-------------|---------|-----------|-----------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 1.20%      | 0.85%   | 0.60%     | 1.50%     |
| S02 (CC)       | 3.80%      | 2.10%   | 2.20%     | 5.10%     |
| S03 (Corp)     | 0.90%      | 0.55%   | 0.45%     | 1.10%     |
| S04 (SME)      | 2.50%      | 1.40%   | 1.30%     | 3.40%     |

### 2.3 Model Validation
- **AUC-ROC**: 0.78 (S01), 0.81 (S02), 0.74 (S03), 0.76 (S04)
- **Hosmer-Lemeshow test**: p-values > 0.05 for all segments → no evidence of poor fit
- **Brier score**: 0.035 (S01), 0.068 (S02), 0.021 (S03), 0.045 (S04)

---

## 3. LGD (Loss Given Default) Estimation

### 3.1 Methodology
- Beta regression model for recovery rates (0–1 bounded)
- Covariates: collateral type, collateral coverage ratio, seniority, jurisdiction, workout time
- LGD = 1 – Recovery Rate

### 3.2 Estimated LGD by Segment

| Segment ID   | LGD (Downturn) | LGD (Expected) | Std Dev |
|--------------|---------------|----------------|---------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 25.0%        | 18.0%          | 8.0%    |
| S02 (CC)       | 85.0%        | 72.0%          | 12.0%   |
| S03 (Corp)     | 45.0%        | 35.0%          | 10.0%   |
| S04 (SME)      | 50.0%        | 38.0%          | 11.0%   |

### 3.3 LGD Model Validation
- **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**: 0.042
- **R-squared (pseudo)**: 0.67
- **Convergence**: All segments converged (gradient < 1e-6)

---

## 4. EAD (Exposure at Default) Estimation

### 4.1 Methodology
- Credit Conversion Factor (CCF) approach for off-balance sheet items
- CCF = (Drawn at default – Current drawn) / (Commitment – Current drawn)
- Linear regression with covariates: utilization rate, product type, facility type

### 4.2 Estimated CCF by Segment

| Segment ID   | Avg CCF | Std Dev | Floor | Cap   |
|--------------|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 100.0%  | 0.0%    | 100%  | 100%  |
| S02 (CC)       | 85.0%   | 12.0%   | 50%   | 100%  |
| S03 (Corp)     | 65.0%   | 18.0%   | 25%   | 100%  |
| S04 (SME)      | 75.0%   | 15.0%   | 25%   | 100%  |

### 4.3 EAD = Drawn + CCF × (Undrawn Commitment)

---

## 5. Concentration Risk Add-On

### 5.1 Methodology: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)

HHI = Σᵢ (EADᵢ / Total EAD)²

### 5.2 Calculation

| Segment | EAD (€M) | Weight | Weight² |
|---------|---------|--------|---------|
| S01     | 2,250   | 0.3266 | 0.1067  |
| S02     | 440     | 0.0639 | 0.0041  |
| S03     | 3,600   | 0.5225 | 0.2730  |
| S04     | 600     | 0.0871 | 0.0076  |
| **Total**| **6,890**| **1.00**| **0.3914** |

**HHI = 0.3914** → High concentration (threshold > 0.25)

### 5.3 Concentration Add-On Factor

Using the Granularity Adjustment (GA) approach:

GA = (1 / N_eff) × PD × (1 – PD) × δ

Where N_eff = 1/HHI = 1/0.3914 = 2.55 effective names

GA = (1/2.55) × 0.015 × 0.985 × 0.35 = **0.202%**

**Concentration capital add-on = 0.202% × Total EAD = €13.9M**

---

## 6. Downturn Calibration

### 6.1 Methodology
- Apply stress multipliers based on historical downturn periods (2008-2009, 2020)
- Downturn PD = PD_PIT × Stress_Multiplier
- Downturn LGD = min(LGD_expected × Stress_Multiplier, 100%)

### 6.2 Stress Multipliers

| Segment ID   | PD Stress Multiplier | LGD Stress Multiplier | Rationale |
|--------------|---------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 3.5×               | 1.4×                 | Housing crash sensitivity |
| S02 (CC)       | 2.0×               | 1.2×                 | Unemployment spike |
| S03 (Corp)     | 4.0×               | 1.5×                 | Default cycle correlation |
| S04 (SME)      | 3.0×               | 1.4×                 | Broader economic stress |

### 6.3 Downturn Parameter Estimates

| Segment ID   | Downturn PD | Downturn LGD |
|--------------|------------|-------------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 4.20%      | 25.0%       |
| S02 (CC)       | 7.60%      | 85.0%       |
| S03 (Corp)     | 3.60%      | 45.0%       |
| S04 (SME)      | 7.50%      | 50.0%       |

---

## 7. Economic Capital Computation

### 7.1 Methodology: Vasicek ASRF Model (Basel IRB)

Capital(K) = EAD × LGD × Φ( (Φ⁻¹(PD) + √R × Φ⁻¹(0.999)) / √(1 – R) ) – EAD × LGD × PD

Where R = asset correlation (function of PD per Basel formula)

### 7.2 Correlation Parameters

| Segment ID   | R (Asset Correlation) | Maturity Adjustment |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| S01 (Mortgage) | 15%                 | 0.85                |
| S02 (CC)       | 4%                  | 1.00                |
| S03 (Corp)     | 24%                 | 1.20                |
| S04 (SME)      | 17%                 | 1.10                |

### 7.3 Economic Capital by Segment (Downturn Parameters)

| Segment ID   | EAD (€M) | PD (DT) | LGD (DT) | Unexpected Loss (€M) | Expected Loss (€M) | Capital (€M) |
|--------------|---------|---------|----------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| S01          | 2,250   | 4.20%   | 25.0%    | 52.7                | 23.6              | 29.1        |
| S02          | 440     | 7.60%   | 85.0%    | 32.7                | 28.4              | 4.3         |
| S03          | 3,600   | 3.60%   | 45.0%    | 108.3               | 58.3              | 50.0        |
| S04          | 600     | 7.50%   | 50.0%    | 28.0                | 22.5              | 5.5         |
| **Total**    | **6,890**|        |          | **221.7**           | **132.8**         | **88.9**    |

**Total Economic Capital = €88.9M (1.29% of EAD)**

### 7.4 Sensitivity Analysis

| Scenario                          | Change          | New Capital (€M) | Δ vs Base |
|-----------------------------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Base Case                         | —              | 88.9            | —         |
| PD +10% (across all segments)     | PD ↑ 10%       | 94.2            | +5.96%    |
| PD –10% (across all segments)     | PD ↓ 10%       | 83.6            | –5.96%    |
| LGD +10% (across all segments)    | LGD ↑ 10%      | 97.8            | +10.01%   |
| LGD –10% (across all segments)    | LGD ↓ 10%      | 80.0            | –10.01%   |
| Correlation +20%                  | R ↑ 20%        | 93.5            | +5.17%    |
| Correlation –20%                  | R ↓ 20%        | 83.9            | –5.62%    |
| Concentration GA × 2              | GA × 2         | 102.8           | +15.64%   |
| Downturn multiplier × 1.25        | Stress ↑ 25%   | 113.6           | +27.78%   |

### 7.5 Key Sensitivity Insights

1. **LGD is the most sensitive parameter** — a 10% increase in LGD drives ~10% increase in capital
2. **PD sensitivity is moderate** — 10% PD change drives ~6% capital change (due to correlation effects dampening the marginal impact)
3. **Correlation sensitivity** is asymmetric — higher correlation increases capital more than lower correlation decreases it
4. **Concentration risk** adds material capital at high HHI levels
5. **Downturn calibration** is the most impactful assumption — the 25% stress multiplier increase adds ~28% more capital

---

## 8. Final Recommendations

1. **Segment-level monitoring**: Track PD and LGD trends monthly at segment level
2. **Back-testing**: Annual back-testing of PD, LGD, and EAD models against realized outcomes
3. **Concentration limits**: Implement single-name and sector concentration limits to keep HHI < 0.25
4. **Downturn refinement**: Update downturn multipliers based on latest macroeconomic stress scenarios (GFC, COVID, 2023 rate hike cycle)
5. **Capital adequacy**: Maintain capital buffer of at least 20% above computed economic capital
6. **Model governance**: Independent model validation, documentation, and approval per SR 11-7 standards

---

*Report generated as part of Model Estimation & Calibration task.*

name: irrbb_governance_roles_and_approvals.md
description: Comprehensive IRRBB governance framework covering committees, roles & responsibilities, sign-off workflow, and escalation procedures.
content:
# IRRBB Governance Roles & Approvals Framework

---

## 1. Committees and Key Roles

| Governance Body / Role | Composition | Primary IRRBB Mandate |
|---|---|---|
| **Board of Directors (BOD)** | Board members (incl. non-executive) | Ultimate oversight of IRRBB; approve policy, risk appetite, and major limits. |
| **Board Risk Committee (BRC)** | Sub-committee of BOD | Review risk appetite, limit framework, and top-level EVE/NII exposures; recommend approval to BOD. |
| **Asset & Liability Committee (ALCO)** | CEO/CFO, Treasurer, CRO, Head of Markets, Business Heads, Head of IRRBB | Manage interest rate risk within approved limits; review NII/EVE projections; approve hedging strategies; monitor limit compliance. |
| **Chief Risk Officer (CRO)** | Independent risk function | Second line of defense; oversee IRRBB measurement, validation of assumptions, and independent challenge. |
| **Finance / Treasury** | Treasurer, Funding & ALM team | First line of defense; execute hedging; manage day-to-day balance sheet positioning; produce NII forecasts. |
| **IRRBB Team / ALM Unit** | Quantitative analysts, risk managers | Perform EVE/NII calculations under standard and internal shocks; run sensitivity analyses; maintain behavioral models. |
| **Internal Audit** | Independent audit function | Third line of defense; periodic review of IRRBB governance, models, and controls. |
| **Model Validation Unit** | Independent from front office and risk modeling | Validate key behavioral assumptions (NMD decay, prepayment) and EVE/NII calculation engines. |

---

## 2. Detailed Responsibilities

### Board of Directors
- Approve the IRRBB Policy, Risk Appetite Statement (RAS), and aggregate EVE / NII limits.
- Review quarterly IRRBB dashboard and ensure alignment with strategic objectives.
- Approve any material exceptions or changes to the risk appetite.

### Board Risk Committee
- Oversee the design of the IRRBB limit framework.
- Review policy annually and recommend updates to the Board.
- Monitor emerging IRRBB regulatory developments (e.g., Basel, EBA, local regulator).

### ALCO
- Meet at least monthly; review EVE and NII under standard +6/+6 parallel shock and alternative scenarios.
- Approve hedging mandates, new product risk limits, and transfer pricing adjustments.
- Escalate limit breaches or material assumption changes to BRC/BOD.
- Review behavioral assumption changes (e.g., NMD repricing, prepayment curves) before implementation.

### CRO (Second Line)
- Provide independent validation of IRRBB metrics and stress tests.
- Challenge assumptions used in EVE/NII calculations.
- Sign off on limit frameworks and policy updates.

### Treasury / ALM (First Line)
- Day-to-day management of interest rate risk positions.
- Calculate NII at risk and EVE sensitivity under required shocks.
- Propose and execute hedge transactions within approved limits.
- Report limit utilization to ALCO.

### IRRBB Team / ALM Unit
- Maintain calculation engine and data infrastructure.
- Run monthly EVE and NII under standard 6 parallel shock scenarios (and any internal scenarios).
- Document behavioral assumptions (core NMD decay, runoff rates, repricing maturities).
- Prepare IRRBB dashboard for ALCO and BRC.

### Internal Audit
- Conduct periodic independent review of IRRBB governance, model risk, and compliance with policy.
- Report findings directly to BRC / Audit Committee.

---

## 3. Sign-Off Workflow

### 3.1 IRRBB Policy & Limit Framework
| Step | Responsible | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | IRRBB Team | Draft policy and limit proposals |
| 2 | CRO / Risk | Review & recommend |
| 3 | ALCO | Approve within delegated authority |
| 4 | BRC | Recommend to Board |
| 5 | BOD | Final approval |
| 6 | Internal Audit | Post-approval review |

### 3.2 Behavioral Assumption Changes (NMD decay, prepayment, etc.)
| Step | Responsible | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | IRRBB Team | Propose change with quantitative impact (EVE/NII delta) |
| 2 | Model Validation | Independent validation |
| 3 | CRO | Concurrence |
| 4 | ALCO | Approval |
| 5 | BRC | Notified (for material changes) |

### 3.3 Derivative Hedging Proposals
| Step | Responsible | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Treasury | Propose hedge structure and notional within limit |
| 2 | CRO | Risk check against limits |
| 3 | ALCO (or delegated) | Approve |
| 4 | Finance | Execute and confirm |
| 5 | Risk | Post-trade limit monitoring |

### 3.4 Monthly / Quarterly IRRBB Reporting
| Step | Responsible | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | IRRBB Team | Prepare EVE/NII report with scenario results |
| 2 | CRO | Review and add commentary |
| 3 | ALCO | Review and discuss actions |
| 4 | BRC | Quarterly review |
| 5 | BOD | Quarterly dashboard for awareness/approval |

---

## 4. Escalation Procedure

### Level 1 – Operational / Limit Breach
- **Trigger**: EVE or NII limits breached (hard limit) or approaching soft limit (>85% utilization).
- **Action**: Treasury notifies CRO and Head of IRRBB within 1 business day.
- **Response**: Remedial plan (e.g., reduce duration gap, execute hedge) within 2 business days. If unaddressed, escalate.

### Level 2 – ALCO Escalation
- **Trigger**: Breach exceeds 3 business days without resolution OR expected to exceed hard limit for >5 days.
- **Action**: ALCO convenes emergency meeting; may approve temporary limit waiver (max 30 days) or require immediate de-risking.
- **Documentation**: Formal waiver memo signed by CRO and Treasurer, noted by BRC.

### Level 3 – Board / BRC Escalation
- **Trigger**: Any breach >30 days, repeated breaches, or failure of a major hedging counterparty / market disruption.
- **Action**: BRC / BOD notified; Board may revise risk appetite or approve capital contingency plan.
- **Communication**: Regulator notification if required by local law/regulation.

### Model / Assumption Escalation
- **Trigger**: Material assumption change (e.g., NMD beta shift >10%, prepayment speed change >20%) with EVE impact >5% of Tier 1 capital.
- **Action**: IRRBB Team presents impact to ALCO within 1 week; ALCO decides on implementation timeline; BRC informed.

---

## 5. Key Principles

1. **Three Lines of Defense**: Operations (1st), Risk Management (2nd), Internal Audit (3rd).
2. **Segregation of Duties**: IRRBB calculation, execution, and validation are separated.
3. **Materiality**: Escalation thresholds align with regulatory significance (e.g., 5% of Tier 1 for EVE).
4. **Timeliness**: Reporting and escalation timelines are clearly defined and tracked.
5. **Documentation**: All sign-offs, waivers, and assumption changes recorded and retained for regulatory inspection.

---

*This framework should be tailored to the institution's size, complexity, and local regulatory requirements.*

name: ICAAP_Risk_Inventory_Materiality_v1.md
description: Comprehensive ICAAP risk inventory covering six risk categories with materiality thresholds, assessment methods, and ownership mapping
content:
# ICAAP Risk Inventory & Materiality Assessment

## 1. Credit Risk

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Default Risk** | Risk of borrower non-payment on loans, bonds, and off-balance-sheet commitments | Loans >€1M individually assessed; portfolios >€50M | IRB Foundation Approach (PD, LGD, EAD); Standardised for non-IRB portfolios | Chief Credit Officer |
| **Concentration Risk** | Single-name and sectoral concentrations exceeding 10% of Tier 1 capital | Single-name >10% Tier 1; sector >25% Tier 1 | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI); Granularity Adjustment | Head of Credit Risk |
| **Sovereign Risk** | Exposure to sovereign debt, particularly high-yield jurisdictions | >5% of total credit exposures | Sovereign risk scoring (Moody's/S&P mapping); stress testing | Head of Country Risk |
| **Counterparty Credit Risk** | Derivative and repo exposures | MtM + CVA >€2M | SA-CCR for EAD; CVA VaR (99%, 1yr) | Head of Counterparty Risk |
| **Residual Risk** | Shortfall between collateral value and exposure | LTV >80% for secured lending | Collateral valuation gap analysis; margin frequency review | Credit Risk Analytics |

## 2. Market Risk & CVA Risk

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Interest Rate Risk (Trading Book)** | Sensitivity to parallel/shaped yield curve shifts | VaR (99%, 10d) >€5M daily average | Historical VaR (2yr lookback); Sensitivity analyses (DV01, key rate durations) | Head of Market Risk |
| **FX Risk** | Open foreign currency positions | Net open position >2% Tier 1 | VaR (99%, 10d); stress scenarios (±20% FX moves) | Head of FX Trading |
| **Equity Risk** | Equity trading positions | VaR (99%, 10d) >€3M | Factor model VaR; idiosyncratic stress tests | Head of Equities |
| **Commodity Risk** | Commodity derivatives and physical positions | VaR (99%, 10d) >€2M | Three-factor model (spot, term, volatility); Monte Carlo | Head of Commodities |
| **CVA Risk** | Mark-to-market loss due to counterparty credit deterioration | CVA VaR (99%, 1yr) >€4M | CVA VaR using CDS-implied PDs; CVA stressed under Basel III FRTB | Head of CVA Desk |
| **Basis Risk** | Mismatch between hedging instruments and underlying exposures | >€0.5M unhedged basis position | Basis point value (BPV) gap analysis; scenario simulation | Market Risk Analytics |

## 3. IRRBB (Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book)

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Repricing Risk** | Mismatch between asset and liability repricing dates | EVE sensitivity >15% Tier 1 | NII simulation (12mo forward); EVE sensitivity (±200bp parallel shock) | Head of ALM |
| **Yield Curve Risk** | Non-parallel shifts in the yield curve | EVE sensitivity >10% Tier 1 for steepener/flattener | Key rate duration analysis; 6 standardised shock scenarios (BCBS 368) | Head of Treasury |
| **Basis Risk** | Imperfect correlation between benchmark rates (e.g., 3M vs 6M LIBOR) | NII impact >€2M per 25bp basis shift | Historical basis correlation analysis; cohort simulation | Treasury Analytics |
| **Optionality Risk** | Embedded options (prepayment, early withdrawal) | Prepayment rate shift >20% of modelled | Option-adjusted spread (OAS) modelling; behavioural prepayment models | Head of Retail Banking |
| **Automatic Interest Rate Options** | Caps, floors, swaptions in banking book | Vega >€0.5M per 1 vol point | Black-Scholes/Monte Carlo for optionality; Greeks sensitivity | ALM Modelling |

## 4. Operational Risk

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Fraud** | Unauthorised activity, theft, misappropriation | Gross loss >€50K per event | Loss Data Collection (internal 5yr history); Risk Control Self-Assessment (RCSA) | Head of Operational Risk |
| **External Fraud** | Cyber-attacks, third-party fraud, theft | Gross loss >€100K per event | Scenario Analysis (worst-case €10M); KRI monitoring (fraud attempt volumes) | Chief Information Security Officer |
| **Employment Practices** | Health & safety, discrimination, employment law | Gross loss >€25K | RCSA; HR incident tracking | Head of HR |
| **Clients, Products & Business Practices** | Mis-selling, market manipulation, fiduciary breaches | Gross loss >€200K or regulatory fine >€1M | RCSA; Product Review Committee oversight; conduct risk indicators | Head of Compliance |
| **Damage to Physical Assets** | Natural disasters, terrorism, infrastructure failure | Gross loss >€500K | Business Continuity Planning (BCP) testing; insurance coverage gap analysis | Head of Facilities |
| **Business Disruption & System Failures** | IT outages, hardware/software failures, telecom loss | Downtime >4hrs or loss >€250K | IT Service Continuity (DR testing); system availability KRIs (99.5% uptime) | Chief Information Officer |
| **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** | Failed transactions, data entry errors, accounting errors | Gross loss >€50K (per event) | KRI (Straight-Through Processing rate); reconciliation exception tracking | Head of Operations |

**AMA Framework**: Advanced Measurement Approach using Loss Distribution Approach (LDA) — frequency (Poisson) and severity (log-normal) convolution; 99.9th percentile VaR over 1yr.

## 5. Concentration Risk

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Name Concentration** | Top 20 borrowers as % of credit portfolio | Sum of top 20 >50% of credit portfolio | HHI; Share-of-portfolio concentration metrics; Gini coefficient | Head of Credit Risk |
| **Sector Concentration** | Exposure by industry (e.g., Real Estate 35%) | Any single sector >20% of total credit | Sector Herfindahl; correlation matrix with systematic factor | Sector Risk Manager |
| **Geographic Concentration** | Country/regional exposure clusters | Single country >15% of total assets | Diversification ratio; cross-country correlation analysis | Head of Country Risk |
| **Product/Instrument Concentration** | Over-reliance on specific credit products | Single product >30% of income or portfolio | Product concentration ratio; scenario impact on product lines | Product Risk Manager |
| **Collateral Concentration** | Over-concentration in single collateral type | Single collateral type >40% of secured exposures | Collateral liquidation value stress (haircuts +20%) | Collateral Risk Manager |
| **Funding Concentration** | Reliance on few funding sources or short tenors | Top 5 depositors >10% of total deposits; LCR <100% | Funding concentration ratio; NSFR; stress liquidity survival horizon | Head of Treasury |

## 6. Model Risk

| Risk Sub-Type | Description | Materiality Threshold | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Data Risk** | Data quality, completeness, timeliness issues | Model outputs vary >5% due to data gaps | Data lineage mapping; completeness checks; back-testing data integrity | Model Risk Manager |
| **Model Specification Risk** | Inappropriate theory, assumptions, or mathematical formulation | Model R² <0.6 or p-values >0.05 for key parameters | Independent model validation (IVU); benchmark model comparison | Head of Model Validation |
| **Calibration Risk** | Parameter instability or estimation error | Parameter drift >20% over 1yr | Sensitivity analysis; parameter stability testing (Chow test); out-of-time validation | Quantitative Analytics |
| **Implementation Risk** | Coding errors, incorrect system integration | Numerical error >1% of model output | Code review; unit testing; parallel run comparison (old vs new) | IT Model Implementation |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Model fit to noise rather than signal | Out-of-sample RMSE >1.5x in-sample RMSE | Cross-validation; regularization (Lasso/Ridge); out-of-sample R² | Model Risk Analytics |
| **Governance Risk** | Inadequate controls, approval deviations, model misuse | Model not approved within materiality tier | Model inventory tracking; model approval governance board | Chief Risk Officer |

**Model Materiality Tiers**:
- Tier 1 (High Impact): >€50M exposure or regulatory capital impact >€10M → Full independent validation annually
- Tier 2 (Medium Impact): >€10M exposure or >€2M capital impact → Independent validation every 2 years
- Tier 3 (Low Impact): Below thresholds → Quantitative assurance and ongoing monitoring

---

## Materiality Assessment Summary

| Risk Category | Gross Exposure (€B) | Net Risk (€B) | Capital Allocation (€M) | Materiality Rating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Credit Risk | 45.2 | 38.6 | 1,245 | HIGH |
| Market Risk & CVA | 12.8 | 8.3 | 425 | HIGH |
| IRRBB | 28.5 (EVE) | 22.1 (EVE) | 380 | HIGH |
| Operational Risk | N/A | N/A | 215 | MEDIUM |
| Concentration Risk | 45.2 (embedded) | 15.2 (surcharge) | 185 | MEDIUM |
| Model Risk | N/A | N/A | 55 | LOW |

**Total ICAAP Capital Requirement**: €2,505M
**Tier 1 Capital Available**: €3,120M
**Capital Buffer (Surplus)**: €615M (19.7% of Tier 1)

All risk categories reviewed and updated as of Q4 2023. Materiality reassessment to be performed semi-annually or upon trigger events (M&A, regulatory change, material loss).

name: reverse_stress_test_framework.md
description: Comprehensive reverse stress test framework including non-viability conditions, pathways, parameterization, and remediation actions
content:
## REVERSE STRESS TEST FRAMEWORK

### 1. Non-Viability Conditions

A firm is deemed non-viable when any of the following threshold breaches occur:

| Condition | Threshold | Metric | Regulatory Basis |
|-----------|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Capital Adequacy Breach | CET1 ratio < 4.5% | Common Equity Tier 1 / RWA | CRR/CRD IV, Basel III |
| Leverage Ratio Breach | Tier 1 Leverage Ratio < 3% | Tier 1 Capital / Total Exposure | Basel III Leverage Framework |
| Liquidity Coverage Breach | LCR < 100% for 30 consecutive days | High Quality Liquid Assets / Net Cash Outflows | Basel III LCR Standard |
| Net Stable Funding Breach | NSFR < 100% | Available Stable Funding / Required Stable Funding | Basel III NSFR Standard |
| Profitability Collapse | 3 consecutive quarters of negative NI | Net Income | Internal Risk Appetite |
| Credit Rating Downgrade | Rating falls below investment grade (e.g., BBB- to BB+) | External Rating | Rating Agency Triggers |
| Concentration Limit Breach | Single-name exposure > 25% of Tier 1 Capital | Exposure / Tier 1 Capital | Large Exposures Regulation |
| Funding Stress | Wholesale funding cost > 500bps over benchmark | Funding Spread | Market-Based Indicators |

### 2. Pathways to Non-Viability

Each pathway describes a plausible sequence of events leading to non-viability.

**Pathway A: Macroeconomic Tail Risk**
- Global recession (GDP -6%, unemployment 12%)
- Sovereign debt crisis in major market
- Double-dip recession with persistent low growth
- Leads to: Widespread corporate defaults, severe credit losses, capital depletion

**Pathway B: Sector Concentration Shock**
- Single-sector downturn (e.g., commercial real estate, energy)
- Sector-specific losses exceed 40% of exposure
- Contagion to correlated sectors via supply chain
- Leads to: Large concentration losses, rating downgrades, funding withdrawal

**Pathway C: Market Dislocation / Systemic Event**
- Sudden risk-off sentiment, liquidity freeze
- Asset price collapse (equities -40%, credit spreads +500bps)
- Counterparty defaults cascade through derivatives
- Leads to: MTM losses, CVA volatility, margin calls, liquidity crisis

**Pathway D: Operational / Conduct Risk**
- Major operational failure (fraud, system outage, cyberattack)
- Regulatory sanctions, fines, business restrictions
- Reputational damage leading to client exodus
- Leads to: Revenue loss, capital fines, elevated funding costs

**Pathway E: Credit Portfolio Deterioration**
- Rapid PD migration across retail and wholesale books
- LGD spiking due to collateral value erosion
- Unexpected correlation between asset classes
- Leads to: Credit losses exceeding EL, PD/LGD stress exceeding VaR cushions

### 3. Parameterization

Key risk factor parameters for simulation:

| Risk Factor | Base Case | Stress Scenario | Severity |
|-------------|-----------|-----------------|----------|
| GDP Growth (%) | 2.0% | -6.0% to -3.0% | Extreme |
| Unemployment Rate (%) | 4.5% | 12.0% | Severe |
| Interest Rates (bps) | 350 | 700 (or 0 in deflation) | Severe |
| Credit Spreads (bps) | 150 | 800+ | Extreme |
| Equity Market (%) | 0% | -40% to -50% | Severe |
| Real Estate Prices (%) | 3% | -30% to -20% | Severe |
| PD (Wholesale, %) | 1.5% | 15% | Extreme |
| PD (Retail, %) | 2.0% | 18% | Extreme |
| LGD (%) | 40% | 70% | Severe |
| Correlation (Asset) | 0.15 | 0.50 | Extreme |

**Loss Estimation Engine:**

Expected Loss = EAD × PD × LGD

Capital Depletion = ΔEL + ΔRWA × Capital Ratio

For portfolio-level stress:

Economic Capital (EC) = VaR_α (Portfolio Loss) - EL

Reverse stress test solves for the factor vector X such that:

P(Loss > Available Capital | X) = 1 (i.e., the firm is non-viable under scenario X)

### 4. Remediation Actions

Each pathway is linked to specific remediation actions with triggers and responsible parties.

| Pathway | Trigger | Immediate Action | Structural Action | Owner |
|---------|---------|------------------|-------------------|-------|
| Macro Tail Risk | GDP < -3%, Unemp > 8% | Capital conservation, dividend suspension | Reduce exposure to cyclicals, raise capital | CRO, CFO |
| Sector Concentration | Sector exposure > 20% of capital | Halt new originations, increase hedges | Portfolio rebalancing, sector limits | Head of Credit Risk |
| Market Dislocation | Spreads > 400bps, VIX > 40 | Reduce trading book VaR, increase collateral | Liquidity buffer expansion, diversification | Treasurer, CRO |
| Operational Risk | Loss event > $50M | Containment, forensic audit | Enhance controls, cyber insurance, BCP | COO, CISO |
| Credit Deterioration | PD > 5% or LGD > 55% | Tighten underwriting, increase provisions | Loan sales, credit derivatives, capital raise | Head of Credit |

**Preventive Measures (always active):**
- Early warning indicators monitored monthly
- Risk appetite limits with hard and soft triggers
- Contingency funding plan 
- Capital buffer maintained above regulatory minimum
- Diversification constraints across sectors, geographies, and products

**Escalation Protocol:**
1. Amber Trigger (warning): Risk committee notified, monitoring intensified
2. Red Trigger (breach): Remediation plan activated, board notified
3. Critical Trigger (near non-viability): Recovery plan executed, regulator notified

### 5. Reverse Stress Testing Process Flow

1. Define non-viability conditions → 2. Identify plausible pathways → 3. Parameterize risk factors → 4. Run reverse simulation → 5. Identify weakest pathways → 6. Link to remediation → 7. Document and report → 8. Review and update annually

---
**Document Version:** 1.0
**Prepared by:** Credit Risk Modeler (Reverse Stress Test Specialist)
**Date:** [Current Date]

content:
## Operational Risk Loss Data Analysis

### 1. Data Overview

A synthetic portfolio of 240 internal operational risk loss events was generated across a 3-year observation window (2022–2024) for a mid-sized financial institution. The dataset includes loss amounts (nominal USD), event dates, and assigned Basel II event type classifications.

---

### 2. Loss Data by Basel II Event Type

| Basel II Event Type (Level 1) | Code | Event Count | Total Loss ($) | Mean Loss ($) | Median Loss ($) | Max Loss ($) | Std Dev ($) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | IF | 28 | 4,820,000 | 172,143 | 95,000 | 1,200,000 | 261,208 |
| External Fraud | EF | 52 | 2,340,000 | 45,000 | 22,500 | 380,000 | 72,141 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | EPWS | 18 | 1,260,000 | 70,000 | 45,000 | 310,000 | 78,994 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | CPBP | 35 | 6,850,000 | 195,714 | 110,000 | 1,850,000 | 351,429 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | DPA | 8 | 720,000 | 90,000 | 65,000 | 280,000 | 88,459 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | BDSF | 22 | 1,980,000 | 90,000 | 52,000 | 520,000 | 120,368 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | EDPM | 77 | 4,390,000 | 57,013 | 28,000 | 610,000 | 96,541 |
| **Total** | **All** | **240** | **22,360,000** | **93,167** | **48,000** | **1,850,000** | **203,418** |

---

### 3. Frequency Analysis

#### 3.1 Annual Frequency per Event Type (Events per Year)

| Event Type | Year 1 (2022) | Year 2 (2023) | Year 3 (2024) | Avg Annual Frequency | Poisson λ Estimate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 9 | 10 | 9 | 9.33 | 9.3 |
| External Fraud | 17 | 18 | 17 | 17.33 | 17.3 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 6 | 6 | 6 | 6.00 | 6.0 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 12 | 11 | 12 | 11.67 | 11.7 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2.67 | 2.7 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 7 | 8 | 7 | 7.33 | 7.3 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 26 | 25 | 26 | 25.67 | 25.7 |
| **Total** | **80** | **81** | **79** | **80.00** | **80.0** |

**Key Observations:**
- **Execution, Delivery & Process Management (EDPM)** is the most frequent event type (~32% of all events), which is consistent with industry benchmarks for retail/commercial banking operations.
- **External Fraud** is the second most frequent (~22%), reflecting typical card fraud, phishing, and cyber-related losses.
- **Damage to Physical Assets** is the least frequent (~3%), consistent with low-probability events.
- Frequencies are stable year-over-year, suggesting a mature control environment with no significant emerging risk trends (Poisson λ parameter is well-estimated).

#### 3.2 Frequency Distribution Fit

For all event types, the Poisson distribution provides a reasonable fit given the stability observed across the three-year window. The variance-to-mean ratio for each type is close to 1.0 (range: 0.89–1.15), supporting the Poisson assumption.

---

### 4. Severity Analysis

#### 4.1 Descriptive Statistics by Event Type

| Event Type | Mean ($) | Median ($) | 75th Percentile ($) | 90th Percentile ($) | 95th Percentile ($) | 99th Percentile ($) | Skewness | Kurtosis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 172,143 | 95,000 | 210,000 | 420,000 | 680,000 | 1,100,000 | 2.8 | 10.5 |
| External Fraud | 45,000 | 22,500 | 55,000 | 95,000 | 150,000 | 320,000 | 3.1 | 14.2 |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 70,000 | 45,000 | 95,000 | 165,000 | 220,000 | 290,000 | 1.9 | 5.8 |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 195,714 | 110,000 | 220,000 | 480,000 | 850,000 | 1,700,000 | 3.4 | 14.8 |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 90,000 | 65,000 | 130,000 | 200,000 | 250,000 | 275,000 | 1.5 | 4.2 |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 90,000 | 52,000 | 125,000 | 210,000 | 310,000 | 480,000 | 2.6 | 9.1 |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 57,013 | 28,000 | 65,000 | 135,000 | 210,000 | 450,000 | 3.0 | 13.5 |

**Key Observations:**
- **Clients, Products & Business Practices (CPBP)** has the highest mean severity (~$196K) and the largest maximum loss ($1.85M), driven by potential litigation, regulatory fines, and mis-selling exposures.
- **Internal Fraud** also shows high severity (mean ~$172K), reflecting the concentrated nature of internal fraud events.
- **External Fraud** has the lowest mean severity ($45K), though it occurs frequently — this is typical for high-volume, low-severity fraud events.
- All event types exhibit positive skewness and high kurtosis (leptokurtic), confirming fat-tailed severity distributions — a hallmark of operational risk.

#### 4.2 Parametric Severity Model Selection

| Event Type | Recommended Distribution | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | LogNormal(μ=11.8, σ=1.2) | Moderate right skew; good Kolmogorov-Smirnov fit (p>0.15) |
| External Fraud | LogNormal(μ=10.5, σ=1.4) | Strong right skew; many small-value events with a few larger outliers |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | LogNormal(μ=11.0, σ=0.9) | Moderate skew; relatively homogeneous severity range |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | LogNormal(μ=12.0, σ=1.5) | High skew; extreme tail risk from litigation/settlement events |
| Damage to Physical Assets | LogNormal(μ=11.2, σ=0.8) | Low skew; relatively bounded severity (limited tail) |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | LogNormal(μ=11.1, σ=1.1) | Moderate skew; IT incidents show clustering around common values |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | LogNormal(μ=10.7, σ=1.3) | Strong skew; manual processing errors create a long tail |

---

### 5. Expected Loss (EL) Estimation

Expected Loss = Annual Frequency (λ) × Mean Severity

| Event Type | λ (events/year) | Mean Severity ($) | Expected Loss ($/year) | % of Total EL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Fraud | 9.3 | 172,143 | 1,600,930 | 17.2% |
| External Fraud | 17.3 | 45,000 | 778,500 | 8.4% |
| Employment Practices & Workplace Safety | 6.0 | 70,000 | 420,000 | 4.5% |
| Clients, Products & Business Practices | 11.7 | 195,714 | 2,289,854 | 24.6% |
| Damage to Physical Assets | 2.7 | 90,000 | 240,300 | 2.6% |
| Business Disruption & System Failures | 7.3 | 90,000 | 657,000 | 7.1% |
| Execution, Delivery & Process Management | 25.7 | 57,013 | 1,465,234 | 15.7% |
| **Total** | **80.0** | **93,167** | **7,453,360** | **100%** |

---

### 6. Concentration Analysis

**Top 3 Event Types by Expected Loss:**
1. **Clients, Products & Business Practices** — 24.6% of total EL
2. **Internal Fraud** — 17.2% of total EL
3. **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** — 15.7% of total EL

**Top 3 Event Types by Frequency:**
1. **Execution, Delivery & Process Management** — 32.1% of events
2. **External Fraud** — 21.7% of events
3. **Clients, Products & Business Practices** — 14.6% of events

**Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for EL concentration:** HHI = 1,658 (moderately concentrated)

---

### 7. Key Risk Indicators (KRIs) and Trends

| KRI | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 | Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Annual Loss ($M) | 7.48 | 7.52 | 7.36 | Stable ✅ |
| Number of Loss Events > $500K | 5 | 6 | 4 | Stable ✅ |
| Average Time to Detection (days) | 42 | 38 | 35 | Improving ✅ |
| Recovery Rate (%) | 8% | 10% | 12% | Improving ✅ |
| Near-miss Events Reported | 120 | 145 | 168 | Improving ✅ |

---

### 8. Conclusions and Recommendations

1. **Focus on CPBP and Internal Fraud** — These two categories account for ~42% of total expected loss. Scenario analysis and model risk overlays should prioritize these areas.
2. **EDPM needs process controls** — While individual losses are moderate, the high frequency of EDPM events suggests automation, system enhancements, or process re-engineering could yield significant risk reduction.
3. **Severity tail risk is material** — The high kurtosis across most event types indicates that extreme losses, though rare, could be severe. Capital allocation models should account for this via the 99.9th percentile VaR in the loss distribution.
4. **Recommend a scenario analysis deep-dive** for CPBP (e.g., regulatory fine scenario of $5M–$10M) and Internal Fraud (e.g., rogue trading scenario).
5. **Model risk overlay**: The severity distribution fits (LogNormal) should be validated against alternative heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., GPD, Log-Gamma) as part of a model risk management framework.
